1: 'Job continued his discourse' — wayyōsep̄ ʾîyyôḇ śēʾt mĕšālô, the same formula as Job 27:1. Chapters 29-31 form Job's final speech — a three-part reflection: Job 29 (the past: what I was), Job 30 (the present: what I am now), Job 31 (the oath of clearance: what I have not done). This is the most formally structured section of Job's dialogues, with a deliberate tripartite architecture. The nostalgic mood of Job 29 is the literary device of the 'golden age' lament, paralleling similar retrospective laments in Sumerian literature ('Lamentation over the Destruction of Ur') and the Psalms (Psalms 42:4; Job 77:5-10: 'I remember the days of long ago').
2: 'As in the months gone by, in the days when God watched over me' — kĕyarḥê qeḏem (as in months of ancient/before), the months of the past. The specific wish is not just for restored circumstances but for restored relationship: 'when God watched over me' (bîmê ʾĕlôah šĕmārānî). šāmar (to watch/guard/keep) is the covenantal protection-verb: God 'watches over' (šāmar) Israel in Numbers 6:24-26 (the Aaronic blessing); Psalms 121:3-8 ('he who watches over you will not slumber'); the same verb of covenant-keeping applies in Proverbs 2:8 and throughout the Psalms. God's 'watching' of Job has become the oppressive surveillance of Job 7:17-20; here Job nostalgically remembers when it was protective.
3: 'His lamp shone on my head and by his light I walked through darkness' — nērô (his lamp); ʾôrô (his light); ḥōšeḵ (darkness). The divine lamp as metaphor for favor, guidance, and life appears throughout: Psalms 18:28 ('you, LORD, keep my lamp burning; my God turns my darkness into light'); Proverbs 6:23 ('a lamp and a light is the law'); 2 Samuel 22:29; the Tabernacle lamp (Exodus 25:37) as the divine light-presence in the midst of the camp. The image of walking through darkness by God's light anticipates the NT's 'your word is a lamp for my feet' (Psalms 119:105) and 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12).
4: 'As I was in the prime of life' (kaʾăšer hāyîṯî biymê ḥorpî) — ḥōrep̄ is the harvest season/autumn, used here as a metaphor for the best years of adulthood (as 'the autumn of life' in some cultures represents mature prime). 'The friendship of God rested upon my tent' — sôḏ ʾĕlôah ʿălê ʾohŏlî. sôḏ means intimate counsel/the secret council (the same word as the divine council in Jeremiah 23:18,22 'stood in the council/sôḏ of YHWH'; Psalms 25:14 'the LORD confides in those who fear him'; Amos 3:7 'he reveals his sôḏ to his servants the prophets'). Job recalls a time when God's intimate counsel rested on his household — the deepest possible description of divine favor.
5: 'When the Almighty was still with me' — bĕʿôḏ Šaddai ʿimmāḏî: the spatial-relational 'with me' (ʿimmāḏî) is the covenant-presence formula: God 'with' his people (Genesis 28:15 to Jacob; Exodus 3:12 to Moses; Joshua 1:5 to Joshua; Isaiah 43:2 'when you pass through the waters I will be with you'). The formula 'with me' expresses the covenantal companionship that Job now experiences as broken. 'My children gathered around me' — the image of children surrounding the patriarch at table (Psalms 128:3 'your children around your table like olive shoots') as the visible sign of covenant blessing.
6: 'I bathed my steps in cream and the rock poured out streams of oil' — reḥōṣ hălîḵāy beḥemāʾ wĕṣûr yāṣûq ʿimmî p̄alğê-šāmen. Two hyperboles of surplus: cream (ḥemʾāh = curds/butter, the richest dairy product) for the feet's path, and oil (šāmen = olive oil) streaming from the rock. These are the paradigmatic foods of covenant blessing in Deuteronomy 32:13-14 ('honey from the rock... curds and milk from herd and flock; with the finest kernels of wheat'). Olive oil flowing from rock is the paradox of abundance that transcends natural limitation — the wilderness rock that flowed with water (Exodus 17:6; Numbers 20:8-11) here flows with oil.
7: 'When I went out to the city gate and took my seat in the public square' — the city gate (šaʿar) as the administrative, judicial, and commercial center of the ancient city (Genesis 19:1; Deuteronomy 21:19; Ruth 4:1-11; Proverbs 31:23). Taking a seat (kûn môšāḇ = establish a seat/position) in the public square (rĕḥōḇ) was the formal act of taking judicial or public-advisory position. The scene Job describes — arriving at the gate to a reception of honor from young men, elders, princes, and nobles — follows the precise social hierarchy of an ancient Israelite city, and will be exactly inverted in Job 30:1-10.
8: The honor-reception sequence: young men step aside (nĕʿārîm rāʾûnî wĕniṯḥabbāʾû = young men saw me and hid/stepped aside), aged men rose and stood (wĕyĕšîšîm qāmû ʿāmādû). In ancient honor culture, the young stepping aside for an elder, and the aged themselves rising from their seats for a distinguished person, were the highest social recognitions. The elders who rise to stand for Job reverse the normal protocol — usually it is youth that rises for age, not the reverse. This hyperbolically describes Job's honor as exceeding even that of the aged sages.
9: 'The princes stopped speaking and covered their mouths with their hands' — śārîm ʿāṣĕrû bĕmillîm = princes restrained their words; kap̄ śāmû lĕp̄îhem = they set their hands to their mouths. The gesture of hand-over-mouth is the ANE posture of awed or respectful silence; it appears in Judges 18:19 (as a command to silence) and is the gesture Elihu says he avoided (29:9b) and the gesture Job himself makes in Job 40:4 ('I put my hand over my mouth') when God speaks from the whirlwind. The princes are silenced not by terror but by reverence for Job's wisdom and authority.
10: 'The voice of the nobles fell silent; their tongues stuck to the roof of their mouths' — qôl nĕgîḏîm neʾĕsāmāh = the voice of the nobles was checked; ûlĕšônām leḥiḵām ḏāḇēqāh = their tongues adhered to their palates. The tongue-adhering-to-palate is an idiomatic expression for complete silence combined with inability to speak (Psalms 22:15 of the sufferer; Lamentations 4:4 of thirsty children). Job uses it to describe the quality of silence his presence commanded: the nobles' normal eloquence was physically arrested. The contrast with Job 30:9 ('and now I am their song and byword') is the sharpest possible social reversal.
11: 'For the ear that heard me called me blessed, and the eye that saw me testified for me' — kî ʾōzen šāmĕʿāh wattĕʾaššĕrenî = the ear that heard me called me blessed; wĕʿayin rāʾǎṯāh wattĕʿîḏēnî = the eye that saw me testified on my behalf. The beatitude-language (ʾišśĕrennî = declared me ʾašrê-blessed, the happiness-formula of Psalms 1:1 and Matthew 5:3-12) and the legal-testimony language (ʿûḏ = to witness/testify) combine: Job's behavior produced both social blessing-acclamation and legal vindication. He was not merely admired but formally attested by those who saw and heard him.
12: 'For I rescued the poor when they cried for help, and the fatherless who had no one to help them' — kî ʾămalleṭ ʿānî mĕšawwēaʿ = I rescued the afflicted man who cried for help; wĕyātôm wĕlōʾ-ʿōzēr lô = and the fatherless who had no helper. The specific trilogy of vulnerable persons — poor (ʿānî), fatherless (yātôm), widow (ʾalmānāh, v13) — is the canonical test-case for social righteousness throughout the OT. Exodus 22:21-24 makes their protection a direct divine concern; Isaiah 1:17 makes it the criterion for authentic religion; and James 1:27 makes it the definition of 'pure and undefiled religion before God.' Job is claiming he passed this test comprehensively.
13: 'The blessing of the one about to perish came to me, and I made the widow's heart sing for joy' — birkat ʾōḇēḏ ʿālay tāḇōʾ = the blessing of the perishing came to me; wĕlēḇ ʾalmānāh ʾărannen = I caused the widow's heart to sing for joy. Two complementary blessings: the active (given by the perishing/rescued person) and the passive (the joy that overflows in the widow's heart). The 'song for joy' (rānan) of the widow's heart is the spontaneous worship-response to justice: the same verb used in Psalms 5:11 ('let them sing for joy') and Isaiah 12:6 ('sing aloud'). Genuine justice creates genuine worship.
14: 'I put on righteousness and it clothed me; my justice was like a robe and a turban' — ṣeḏeq lāḇaštî wayyilbāšēnî = I put on righteousness and it clothed me; kĕmĕʿîl ûṣānîp̄ mišpāṭî = my justice was like a robe and a turban. The vestment metaphor for righteousness is significant: clothing connotes identity, social role, and consecration. The robe (mĕʿîl = the priestly or royal outer garment; 1 Samuel 24:4; Ezra 9:3; the high priest's robe in Exodus 28:4) and turban (ṣānîp̄ = the priestly or royal head-turban; Exodus 29:9; Zechariah 3:5; Isaiah 62:3) make Job's righteousness priestly-royal in its quality. The NT develops this: Isaiah 61:10 uses the same robe/garland imagery for the redeemed; Revelation 19:8 ('fine linen stands for the righteous acts of God's holy people').
15: 'I was eyes for the blind and feet for the lame' — ʿênayim hāyîṯî laʿiwwēr wĕraglayim lappissēaḥ ʾānî = I was eyes for the blind man, and feet for the lame man. This verse stands at the convergence of OT social ethics and Messianic prophecy. Isaiah 35:5-6 describes the age of salvation: 'then the eyes of the blind will be opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped; then the lame will leap like a deer.' When John the Baptist sends disciples to ask Jesus 'are you the one who is to come?', Jesus answers by citing precisely these signs: 'the blind receive sight, the lame walk, the deaf hear, the dead are raised' (Matthew 11:4-5; Luke 7:22). Job's righteous ministry embodies the pattern that the Messiah will fulfill eschatologically.
16: 'I was a father to the needy, and I investigated the case of the one I did not know' — ʾāḇ ʾānōḵî lāʾeḇyônîm = I was a father to the destitute; wĕrîḇ lōʾ-yāḏaʿtî ʾeḥqĕrēhû = and the case of one I did not know I investigated. The 'father of the poor' is a royal and priestly title in the ANE: Egyptian pharaohs and Mesopotamian kings described themselves as 'fathers of the poor' in royal ideology. Job's self-description as father of the destitute places him in this tradition of responsible power. The legal investigation (ḥāqar = to research/investigate, same root as the wisdom-research of Job 28:27) of unknown cases shows proactive justice: Job did not wait for cases to come to him.
17: 'I broke the fangs of the wicked and forced the prey from his teeth' — ʾăšabberāh mĕṭallĕʿôṯ ʿawwal = I broke the fangs of the unjust man; ûmin-šinnāyw ʾašlîḵ tāref̄ = and I threw the prey from his teeth. The predator-imagery for the oppressor (wicked man as lion holding prey in its teeth) and the justice-intervention as forced-restitution is vivid. The lion-fang metaphor appears in Psalms 3:7 ('strike all my enemies on the jaw; break the teeth of the wicked'); the active justice of forcibly removing what the powerful have taken from the vulnerable is the anti-prey logic of Amos 1-2 (the prophetic indictments of nations for oppression).
18: 'Then I said: I shall die in my own nest and multiply my days like the sand' — ʾāmartî ʿim-qinnî ʾegwaʿ = I said: in my nest I shall expire; wĕkĕḥôl ʾarbeh yāmîm = and as the sand I shall multiply my days. The nest (qēn = nest, used for birds' nests and human homes as places of security and children; Proverbs 27:8; Numbers 24:21; Isaiah 10:14) as image of secure old-age death. The word ḥôl (usually 'sand') has generated significant ancient interpretation: the Septuagint and some Aramaic Targums read it as 'phoenix' (the mythological bird that dies and rises from its ashes), giving the verse a resurrection connotation. The Phoenix legend was known in Egypt and Greece; some ancient readers understood Job here to be expressing hope in a post-mortem renewal.
19: 'My roots spread out to the waters, and the dew rested all night on my branches' — šōrĕšî p̄āṯûaḥ ʾĕlê-māyim = my root opens/spreads to the waters; wĕṭal yālîn biqṣîrî = dew lodges in my branch. The tree-root-to-water image is the standard biblical metaphor for the righteous person firmly sustained by God: Psalms 1:3 ('like a tree planted by streams of water which yields its fruit in season'); Jeremiah 17:8 ('he will be like a tree planted by the water that sends out its roots by the stream'). Job's self-description as a water-connected, dew-bedewed tree captures the ideal of continuous divine sustenance that characterized his former life.
20: 'My glory remained fresh within me, and my bow was always renewed in my hand' — kĕḇôḏî ḥāḏāš ʿimmāḏî = my glory fresh with me; wĕqaštî bĕyāḏî taḥălîp̄ = my bow in my hand renewed. The bow (qešeṯ) as symbol of military power and social authority: Genesis 49:24 uses it for Joseph ('his bow remained steady'); the high priest's installation involved weapons as well as robes. 'Always renewed' (taḥălîp̄ = continually changes/renews; from ḥālap̄ = to pass through/renew) describes continuous rejuvenation rather than static possession — Job's power was not a fixed asset but constantly refreshed. The contrast with Job 30's complete enfeeblement will be total.
21: 'Men listened to me; they waited and held their silence for my counsel' — lî-šāmĕʿû wĕyiḥēllû = for me they listened and waited; wĕyiddĕmû lĕmôʿāṯî = and fell silent for my counsel. The waiting (yāḥal = to hope/wait) and silence (dāmam = to be still/silent) before Job's speech captures the reverential anticipation of the sage's audience. In the wisdom literature, the ideal student is one who listens attentively and waits for the full teaching before speaking (Proverbs 18:13 'to answer before listening is folly and shame'). Job's followers embodied this ideal — their silence for his counsel was the highest tribute a wisdom-teacher could receive.
22: 'After my words they spoke no more; my speech fell upon them' — ʾaḥărê ḏĕḇārî lōʾ yišnû = after my words they did not repeat/speak again; wĕʿālêhem tittôp̄ mĕlātî = and upon them my word dripped. tāṭap̄ (to drip/distill; used in Deuteronomy 32:2 for teaching that 'distills like the rain' and 'drips like the dew'; Amos 9:13 for wine dripping from the mountains). Job's speech is described not as thunderclap-authority but as gentle penetrating drip — the image of wisdom that soaks in slowly but permanently, like rain into soil.
23: 'They waited for me as for the rain and opened their mouths wide as for the spring rain' — wĕyiḥallû kammāṭār lî = and they waited for me as for rain; ûp̄îhem pāʿărû lĕmalqôš = their mouths they opened wide for the late/spring rain (malqôš). The malqôš (the spring/latter rain of March-April, the final rainfall before the dry summer harvest season) is the critical agricultural rain whose presence or absence determined the year's crop success. To wait 'as for the late rain' describes the most intense, survival-level longing. Job's wisdom-speech was to his community what the late rain was to ancient Palestinian agriculture: the word of life that determined whether the crop would survive.
24: 'When I smiled at them they could hardly believe it; they did not let the light of my face fall' — ʾesḥaq ʾălêhem lōʾ yaʾămînû = I smiled at them and they could not believe it; wĕʾôr pānay lōʾ yappîlûn = and the light of my face they did not cast down. Two readings are possible: (1) Job's smile of approval was so rare and precious that when it came people could hardly believe their good fortune; (2) Job smiles and people are overwhelmed with unexpected honor. 'The light of my face' (ʾôr pānay) echoes the Aaronic blessing: 'YHWH makes his face shine upon you' (Numbers 6:25; Psalms 4:6; Job 44:3; Job 80:3,7,19); Job's face-light was experienced as divine-blessing analog by his community.
25: 'I chose the course for them and sat as their head; I lived as a king among his troops, as one who comforts those who mourn' — ʾeḇḥar darqām wĕʾēšēḇ rōʾš = I chose their way and sat as head; wĕʾeškôn kĕmelek baggĕḏûḏ = I dwelt as a king in the troop; kaʾăšer ʾăḏar ʾăḇēlîm yĕnaḥēm = as one who comforts mourners. The chapter closes with Job as both king and comforter — an unusual and significant combination. 2 Corinthians 1:3-4 describes the God of all comfort who comforts us so we can comfort others; Isaiah 61:2 commissions the servant 'to comfort all who mourn.' Job's self-portrait as mourners'-comforter is the personal enactment of what will become the servant's eschatological commission — and what Jesus claims as his own in Luke 4:18-19.
